speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: This conversation with Pearl Maclied and Florence Melder Lange took place in Ms. McLeod's home in Moscow, Idaho on May the 1st, 1978. The interviewer is Rachel Foxman.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Okay.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now you want me to go ahead with this effort here?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think maybe I should. Well, what I did, you see, I wrote up your Encouraged by Dr. Gritman. As I said, I didn't have to know that, but the year is 1892. Yeah, she can get that too. Well, now some of these things were not, they were some that Mrs. Gritman told me when Dr. Gritman, he had gone to England and Vienna and had studied over there. And these were some of the things that they were using over there, which they were not using over here. They were doing major surgeries.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: This conversation with Pearl McLeod and Florence Melder -Lange took place in Ms. McLeod's home in Moscow on May the 1st, 1978. The interviewer is Rachel Foxman.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And of course everybody had to wear a mask because that was the time of the flu epidemic we had. And now Dr. Gritman, he had, when I came here, they had their own cows. And they also had, he also had two teams of horses. Two teams. And during this epidemic, he would have drivers. He wouldn't drive because he would be so tired he would be sleeping because he would make so many country calls because there was no room in the hospital for all the patients who were ill.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How large was the hospital at that time? Do you know?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I don't, there was so much space taken up by the people who worked there and lived in there. So I don't think it was any larger patient beds with the wards and everything. I don't think we had more than 30 beds. I think that would be ample to say that. Yes, 30 beds would be sufficient.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How large was the staff that was living there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That was living there. Well, it, it...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I can't remember how many nurses they had all the time, you see. And some would be there for a certain length of time and then they would quit. They decided they didn't want it. Even some of them, before their three months were up, because they had three months of probation, you could decide whether you want to be a nurse or for something else. And, of course, Dr. Bing, having been over there, he had a hard time getting back just during the war, but he finally made it back after 1914 or along in there. Of course, I came in 1918. And at that time, now the nurses made all the dressings for the offices and the hospital. And at that time we had a steam sterilizer, but it was not under pressure. It was similar to a, to a cooker at the bowl.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: What do you call those? A pressure cooker?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, it wasn't a pressure cooker. There wasn't any...a double boiler. That's what I want to say. It was on that simmer, but yet there was steam that came, you know, clear up through the hole. And this thing was quite large. And the water, of course, was all in the bottom. And it was made in such a way that the water could come up all around the whole, the whole thing. And then we sterilized. Of course, we have to make all these things first. And then we sterilized our materials. They were sterilized for two days. They were sterilized an hour each day. A third day they were sterilized for three hours. Then they were dried and were ready for use. That was for the whole hospital.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And the three hour period was to, I guess I'd say annihilate, annihilate the germs that were called spore bearing germs. And they were much harder to dispose of than any other type of germ. And then in 19...about 1921, the doctor had bought all of this equipment. It was all set up in his surgery. But Washington Water Power had not yet been able to connect it. I don't know if their lines weren't heavy enough or what the problem was. In 1921, it was all connected up.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And then we could sterilize with 250 pounds of pressure. And you could sterilize about 30 minutes, 20 to 30 minutes. Some of the things, some of the materials you didn't need to sterilize quite as long as some of the others.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Now would this be dressing plus the equipment?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It would be dressings for the whole hospital and plus the offices. And all of the materials we would use in the surgery and anything we had to use for the maternity cases.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Such as?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you...and perineal pads for maternity cases. And you had to have some pads sometimes to put on the nipples to get your nipple. And for that, the nurses would make up an ointment of Vaseline and Bismuth. And you worked as much Bismuth in this Vaseline that you could, then you would apply that to the nipples. And you always had to take it off before your baby went. That's half of them, yes. Yes, and it could help heal them up. Because so many times you had a cracked nipple.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What were the dressings?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you need sponges of all types in a hospital.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Gauze?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, yes. We would buy ten bolts of gauze and there would be a hundred yards in each bolt at a time. And cotton. And various things. However, now at that time you had no plaster. You didn't have any elastic bandages. And so, but, doctor did have an x -ray. And he would x -ray his fractures. And then he would, the laundry had, had always on hand three different types of starch. Three different strengths, you might say. So he could tell what strength of this starch he would want for a certain fracture to make a cast. Now you can know how, if you have a heavy starch, and by the, we did have muslin, muslin bandages, but not any fine bandages like you have now. Any gauze bandages. We didn't have any of those. And we also used plain cardboard. You cut that, any strip you wanted, or any size he would want, and soak it in water until it was very pliable. And then he would use that and bandage that on arms or for children. And when that dried, you had a good cast.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So, and you had, didn't have any adhesive. Of course, for a cast you don't need the adhesive. But some of the dressings you put on, you have to, you have to fasten your end down, you know. So he, I never saw anybody who could use just a small stick pin to fasten that end down as well as he did, and never came out. Those kids didn't get it, didn't pull it out or anything. Just for real.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, have we covered everything about the surgery that we need on the kids?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you didn't tell that you boiled the instruments. Oh, well, at first, yes, we did boil the instruments. We boiled the gloves. And we boiled a couple of pans that we used.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How long would you have to boil them for?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, 20 minutes is fine. Especially if you have a cover over them. Otherwise, if you take one pan and cover it with the other pan, then you can handle it pretty well without, you know. Yes, we didn't dry sterilize our gloves for quite a while after we had the autoclave.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Oh, didn't you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, not right at first. It was just, in fact, you know, it was an experiment for all the nurses. They had used anything like that. And, but then I guess they had instructions what to do with it. And coming to the food for the patients. Now, Mrs. Gritman, she planned the meals. And she also kind of saw, did the buying for, you know. She did the buying of all the foods and that sort of thing. And she took care of all the dairy products, all the milk.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did the milk come from the cows that they had?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: We had them, had a janitor who did the milking. And took care of the teams for a doctor because he would come back when this epidemic was on. He would come back and he would have to have his medicine bag replenished. And they would change teams then. Because I guess one team was tired.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Did you want to add that some of the food came in payment of a bill?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, yes. Some of the food was... People who had... People who couldn't pay with it. But now, as far as the nurses training was concerned, we did not have coffee breaks or we did not have any... There was no smoking. In fact, they didn't take in any nurses who smoked. Well, not very many women were smoking cigarettes. Not at that time, no. Now, when we get to the obstetrics, the babies were delivered in the patient's rooms. And it wasn't very easy because your beds were kind of low.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And then after your baby was delivered, it was taken care of.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Say, do you remember where did we take care of when we had all the baby's rooms full? Where did we give that baby's first bath?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I remember we gave it right in the nursery. We took the baby right to the nursery. I didn't think we had a nursery right to begin with. Well, you had a nursery by the time I came. The youth got there. To me, it seems like we put it... We took it into that little kitchen, diet kitchen we had there on the second floor. Where it was warm. Where it was warm. We had a cold stove in there. We always had to take a lot of water.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: For what?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I don't know. We couldn't make any coffee. We had a little kitchen. And then there was a shelf in this room. In this little kitchen. And then we laid this baby on the shelf where anybody, the nurses, could always see to it when they got into this. The baby wasn't alone at any time for several, at least eight hours after it was born. And then we put it in the plain, closed that with the caterpillar. And put it back in the mother's room. The babies were always with the mothers. But they were in the nursery when you came. But at first when I got there, why they were not. They were in the... When I was cleaning the rooms, I had already told her that. When I cleaned the rooms and things, they were with the mothers in there.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it just one mother to a room?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Just one mother to a room, yes. We only had wards on the third floor. And there we only had two wards there. Beyond Mrs. Gritman's room, as you would come up there to go out onto that little porch, there was one room there that had two beds in. Beyond, as you went around the stairs there. On the first floor. On the second floor. There were two beds in that one room. But we hardly ever used that one room. Because it was kind of away from the nurse's station. You can figure it out now once you're in that room. Well, you went clear around as if you were going upstairs.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But then that doesn't matter at all. We used it very little. Very little for anything.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Did they use to...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It seems to me, as I understand it, now when babies are born, they do the treatment on the eyes for syphilis. Oh, we did that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did that too?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, we put at that time, all they had for baby's eyes was Argyrol. And we used that at that time. And of course, at that time, babies had so many more clothes to wear. I mean, we put so many more clothes on them. We put bands and shirts and... Diaper and then your sleeping gown. But now all they put on them is a diaper and a shirt.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: They had bands?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, just a band here to keep the navel dressing on. Oh. Yeah, because we didn't have any adhesive to stick it on. This would be a good time to tell that we had an overload of babies in the nursery. And so this one little fella came along and there wasn't any basket for him. And so at that time, we had to... We opened the dresser drawer that was in there and we made him a little bed in the dresser drawer. And there was a funny in the funny paper that this little KO his name was, always slept in the dresser drawer. And so we called this little baby KO.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, and then also, I'll tell about this... Tell about the little twins right now. This is quite a while afterward. I was very concerned that these twins, this mother came in and she had one baby. But she had developed a very acute heart condition. And she was pregnant. And of course at that time, Dr. Armstrong was here. Dr. Gritman had passed away. And Dr. Armstrong suggested to the mother to have an abortion because he didn't think that she could live and have another baby because her heart was so bad. Well, the mother -to -be didn't want an abortion and neither did her mother. So the doctor said, well, I guess we have to do the best we can, watch you very closely. But at seven months, she delivered twins, mind you. And one weighed two and a half pounds and the other one weighed two and three quarter pounds. We had no incubators or anything like that. So we put these babies in baskets and put hot water bottles around them. And of course they wouldn't nurse. They were too weak to nurse. And so we inserted a very small tube down into the stomach and fit them that way every two hours. And sometimes we would drop a drop or two of water in their mouth. Sometimes they would swallow it and again they would just have drawn out. And those babies lived. And the mother, when the children were about six years old, the mother died of her heart condition. But the grandmother then took the children.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Do you remember what the name of these people were?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Berts. Berts. Oh, Berts Gaffay. That was evidently his first name I presume. I don't know. Well, now the girl that had the children, she was married. Her name was different. Yes, her name was Berts Gaffay. And she was living down here. They took them home down to Clarkston then. They were living down at Clarkston. And the grandmother brought the children up after the mother had died for us to see.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I guess I'm surprised to hear that a doctor would advise abortion. I guess I was under the impression that that was never done. Oh yes, if the mother's life was in danger, why they would advise abortion.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But it was rare. It would have been considered a therapeutic abortion. That's what you have to be down there, therapeutic abortion. Or as the mother's life was in danger. And it was.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How would they induce an abortion at that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, it would be easier at that time than later on.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: But did they give like a medication?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, it has to be done surgically.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it done surgically?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it like a technique they would use today? Well, it's a karatman, isn't it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, it's a regular karatman, karataj. But I understand. Now I haven't been, remember, I haven't been in the hospital since the fall of 1935. That is, I haven't worked in a hospital. But I understand. Now I was talking to a salesman the other day. He just came up to visit me because they hadn't seen me for a while since I had been out in nursing home, you know. So, in some way or other, we got on to this abortion thing. And, but he told me that they had regular sets made up.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Did they have saline in them?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They have awash, yeah, awash and everything in them. That he said, you do it, they get these sets and you do it early and there's nothing to it. You just wash everything out. And of course I didn't know that because I don't... I didn't know they had these sets. Yeah, he said they had sets and there was everything in there and all your stuff was disposable.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, why don't we set up a practice?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And all of your stuff was disposable. Everything was disposable. Now you see, when I was nursing and was in training and everything, now we didn't throw anything away that we could use a second time.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Nothing.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But the hospital being owned by one person, that person was responsible for everything, you know. And naturally, if you work for one person, you're going to be saving more. Now, I think you go into some hospitals and some nurses are just terribly wasteful. And that's the reason that it is so expensive, I think, that it's become so expensive. You don't hear of any private hospitals anymore. Not at all.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: But at that time there were quite a few private hospitals?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, now Moscow had a Dr. Gritman's hospital. However, he would not let anyone else come in there and work and practice. If they were in there, he hired them. He put them on a salary. However, he had a friend in Pullman, a Dr. McGuire. He let him bring his patients over here. And he was a big help many times. He was at the hospital in the morning when the surgery was going on, and he would give the anesthetic. Otherwise, they would have a nurse give the anesthetic. Which was Dr. Gritman. At first, the patient would be heavily sedated before he went to surgery. He walked. He wasn't out as much, as though he couldn't walk. He walked up the steps with, you know, a person on each side of you. And then, Dr. Gritman himself, he had been scrubbed and could have been ready to operate. Then he himself started an anesthetic like this, chloroform, which acts very fast. And it's a little easier to take than ether.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How was it administered?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Drip.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Mask?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You put a mask and then you just drip. And you have to be careful. In putting a mask over a patient who's going to have chloroform, you have to not let it touch his face, and you have to be assured that it's going to get some air. And don't drip any chloroform anyplace because it burns. It makes a burn. Well, they don't even allow... We don't have it at the hospital anymore at all.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. It isn't used anymore. But it can be used if you know how to use it. Yes, but then they don't... You know, we used to clean different things with chloroform. But they don't... And nor do they have ether. And just as soon as they'd had a little of this, just enough so they didn't know what was going on, then Dr. Gritman would start to drip ether. And then he would put the patient practically under and then he would hand it over to whoever was going to give the anesthetic.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So they'd administer chloroform and then ether?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And then another kind of anesthetic?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Just the two?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, the patient had a hypo that was very effective. They had morphine and a high -cine morphine and a cactoid. That was given before the patient went up to surgery.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Why would they need to use three types of...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, that's one thing that... Because in the first place, you don't want to give enough chloroform to do major surgery because it has a bad effect on the liver and that sort of thing. In fact, you can kill a person with this very easily. Very, very easily. Did many people die when they were first... We didn't have any because we didn't use enough of it. Just enough so the person didn't... Because it acted quickly.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's the only reason we used it.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why couldn't you just give them ether?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Because ether is not very pleasant if you start out... You could start out with ether, but it's not pleasant to take. This is much more pleasant to take and knocks you out. It's effective much faster. But you can. But one place you cannot use ether, and that's in a home where you have no electric... Where your lighting is not electric.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You cannot use ether? Where there's any fire?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Where you have any kind of flame, you cannot use ether. And now we haven't gone to the country yet to do deliveries. No, we haven't gone to the country yet. But you go ahead and...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Where was I?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You tell about the home deliveries.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And you didn't take the patient down the stairs on the collapsible stretcher that frightened us all to death. Explain the collapsible stretcher. I don't know about how many stairs, but you understand this patient walked up to the second floor to the surgery. But he was unable, of course, to walk down. So he was loaded onto the stretcher by the doctor and the nurses and whoever was there. We had a strong man that was with the patient, why, he would help. The stretchers would collapse if you touched them here in the... You only had to... Hold the handle. Yeah, you only had to hold the handles. And the main part of the stretcher where the patient was lying on, it was made of very heavy canvas. It doubled maybe three times. Three layers, you know, of canvas. And then as soon as you put your handles on, which you could see... Now, I don't know much about machinery. They had... They folded up. They folded up. Here's the center of your stretcher, right there. And then your handles came out like that, you see. Opened out. Opened out. And as long as you had a hold of your handle here at the end, you were safe.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But if anybody came along, why?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It would open.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And it collapsed. It would collapse. If you took a hold of it in the wrong place, it would collapse. Your patient or whatever.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did that happen many times?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Never happened with us, but it happened with two doctors that came in there afterward. Because they just didn't realize that you... You weren't doing it too fast in the first place. You thought they were going to a fire with that patient.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And they never did understand it, so they just quit using it. Well, it was a burden to carry that down. It was. I have carried the bend of that. If the patient wasn't too heavy, I carried the end of it a lot stronger.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So you would assist in the surgery as a nurse?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. Well, as long as Dr. Gritman was there, I was obviously in the surgery. And one other nurse would be in there too. However, I usually would handle the instruments and the other girl would take care of the sutures. Dr. Gritman always had two nurses. Most of the time. If they were available, you know. Sometimes something else was going on downstairs and he couldn't have two in the surgery. But he liked to have two of them and he always liked to have, after I graduated, he always liked to have one who was still in training. So she'd be learning all the time. But you see, by the time I came, well, Dr. Armstrong would assist or... And to me, or to him, we had two bags. And in one bag, there was a... A pan was fixed in there with a lid on and everything and it just slid in the bottom of the bag. And we always boiled our instruments and everything before we knew that we were going out. But the other bag was always ready to go. And you got there, you saw the patient was ready, why, you set it up. And usually we found a pan or something around the kitchen that we would boil in case we needed some sterile water or something.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So, the women, there was sometimes always a neighbor woman there. And she had always had baked some... She had made some little squares of muslin, old sheets you might say, or any oldish tile or whatever you had. And she had taken that, wrapped it in paper and baked it in the oven. I remember that. For, to put a dressing on the navel when you had your new baby. And of course she had fixed also some perineal pads for the mother to wear, you see, out of these old sheets. And she had baked those in the oven. And lots of times they just had kept a warm... Now they always had, you always had a cook stove in the country. I don't know of any place I ever was that didn't have a cook stove in the kitchen. And they had a pan on the back of the stove which was warm, Lysol solution. And they would dip their cloth in this Lysol solution and then take the ends of it and wring it real tight. And then put it on the mother for a perineal pad and then put a dry one over it. And of course we cleaned up the baby and dressed it and left everything, left enough medicine for the mother to take. And they always got up, they always had to get up to use the commode. Oh yes. So you see, even they got up earlier at that time than they do now. And that they did at my time. Yes. They stayed in bed for, oh I was in bed for ten days and never let you up. Yeah, but these ladies in the country, they had to get up. They got up and used the commodes. And some of them we had to let them up even in the hospital because they absolutely could not avoid on the commodes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why would they keep, women, why would they tell them you have to stay in bed for ten days?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well it was just the times when they thought that you were very weak and all your organs must go back into place. And that sort of thing. They had, you see we had binders that we would buy and put on there real tight so as to get them back in shape. And they'd get their girlish figure back. But those binders felt good because they supported their backs too, you know. And we put binders on our surgical patients too. They were easier to handle in bed. You could grab a binder, you know, and help turn it over and things like that. It would make you ever last and you'd get them back into shape and pinning on them. Now this is all that's taking place except about this doctor, let me see what I have.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Let's see, have I covered everything for him pretty much?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Except I want to... I want you to tell about his proctoclysis equipment. Oh, well I want to also say about the surgery. Now he had a lot of surgical, I mean, ruptured appendix because people didn't come to the doctor when they first got sick. They waited until they were really sick. So he had, we had many ruptured appendix. And now you didn't have any, any, uh, tetracycline, what do you call a, antibiotics. He didn't have any antibiotics to give. So he put in a lot of drains. Sometimes he would put in ten drains in one person. And your drain was made of a material that at the time we called it the percha, which was, gauze was wrapped around it. And then of course there were pins linking them together so you wouldn't lose one, you know. But after we took the drains out, some patients started to smell terrible due to all this infection. Then he let us irrigate that incision with a chlorosine solution, which did away with the smell for several hours. And he had us do that all the time until that patient was well.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: I don't think I understand how the drain would, would there just be an incision and then this would be put in? And then it would just flow out?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, no, it wouldn't blow out, it stayed in there. She meant flow out. Oh no, the drainage would come through the drainage, through this tube onto your dressings. Yeah, it would drain through those drains.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What would make it drain because there was so much infection there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yeah, ..., infection in there. I have seen, that's what makes the odor. I have seen, one man especially, he came in there and he was so distended with his infection and everything. He had ruptured..., but he had accumulated so much pus, actually pus, that he looked like a person about seven months pregnant. And you can't imagine how much pus was, when he was opened up, how much he took out. And then of course you have to put in a lot of drains because it keeps coming out, keeps coming out all the time. And did Dr.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Gritman shorten the drains like ever? Did he change the dressings like they do?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Sometimes he would pull it up and cut it off, you know. And sometimes he would take out one, and that's the way it went, you know. Because that patient was there about six weeks in the hospital. And we lost very few, very few. We lost patients that had a gangrenous appendix, which meant that appendix was... gangrene. Yeah, that gangrene had already died. It was attached to a live person, but it was dead.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And of course, when Dr. Gritman operated, there was no talk going on in the surgery. Only what just had to be.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: About on average, how many people would he operate on in a day?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, well, some days you wouldn't have any. And some days you perhaps would have a couple of major surgeries and a couple of tonsils, you know. And some days he would do just tonsils, perhaps he would do three or four tonsils. And wouldn't have any major surgery. And, you know, you didn't have a baby every day either in a hospital that small.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was he doing another, just a regular practice other than surgery, too?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yes, his office was right downstairs. And he had, he saw office patients all day long until he was needed upstairs for delivery or something of that sort. And of course, he was right there in the hospital all the time. He lived in the hospital. He and Mrs. Gritman both. They had a big apartment in the hospital. He lived in the hospital for the night he went. That first night he went away to his home. When they were going to retire, he died. Now he was going to quit practicing because he had heart condition. And he had had this heart condition. I think he had it when I came. Because I see him take these little pills occasionally, you see. So I knew, later on, I knew what he was doing. That he had this heart condition.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, let me see. Oh yes, x -ray. Now he had an x -ray. And I'm sure it was the only one in town.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And I don't know, have you ever seen x -ray film that they use now?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You have. Well, your first x -ray films, they were just like a window pane, except they were painted green. And you took your picture on this. And he took his first picture and saw that he had a fracture and everything. And he fixed it up and he didn't take any more pictures because he didn't feel as necessary. Because those older doctors, they could feel their fractures. Much more so than, of course now the doctors, they have to. They have to protect themselves.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did he have the x -ray machine when you went to work there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: He had it when I came to the hospital.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Which was what year?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I came there in 1918, but I didn't go into nurses training until 1921. Because I didn't think I'd had enough money yet. Because I have to start from scratch, like a lot of people do. But don't stay with it. There's quite a number of nurses who came there and started training even while I was there. And then, some would stay for six months, eight months, you know. And then decide they didn't want any product.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So that your first years coming here between 1918 and 1921, what was your job in the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, at that time, I did the cleaning. I cleaned the patients' rooms and everybody had long hair. At that time, those days, I combed their hair, I took care of their flowers and anything. And then I also took care of the halls, cleaned the halls. And then helped. I helped any place where they needed some help.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did that job have a title?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, well you might say. Now they call it the nurses' aid. But at that time, I was nothing. The nurses' aid, now they give baths and do that sort of thing. Yes, uh -huh. But I didn't do that. Yours were all non -nursing. Yes, I passed wash water surveillance with patients in the mornings. Could wash their face and help brush their teeth, you know, and that sort of thing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were you in uniform?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I was wearing just what we used to wear in surgery. Just a gown with a very short sleeve, you know, just a straight dress.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were they long?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, no, no, they weren't long. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, what were your, then when you started training, what were your dresses? Were they striped or were they checked?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Blue. They were blue.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Blue striped, but not striped?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, we just had blue dresses and white aprons. That's what... I'm going to get some of those pictures. I think she'll get a good idea of the uniform and stuff.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: What did you say about the uniform?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I'm not going to ask you some. Well, I was just going to say that she had done this, these jobs that she described to you in, before, when she came, you see, in this period of 1918. Then for two years she did this. So when she had her, Dr. Gritman encouraged her in 1921 to take nurses' trainings. And so at that time then she started, but now we all had probationary periods, but she didn't have to have because she was well acquainted with all the duties of a probationer. So she started right in with her nurses' training.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How long would that training last?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: She trained from 1921 to 1924. Three years. Uh -huh. And then during that time you went on duty at 7 o 'clock in the morning and then you had your half hour for noon lunch and they served you right down in the dining room there. And if, of course, she would be busy watching, for instance, watching an OB, why then maybe she'd just have something brought up. She didn't get to have her lunch. But on her two hours off, that was when she studied. You still had to do, read the books. You still had to study.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Shall I go ahead and give her the name of the books?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, let's go see. She still had to study. But before this time that she went into her training, she spent two hours a day up at the... Ursuline Academy. At the academy for private tutoring to make the equivalent of her high school subjects. And then, of course, after she was in nurses training, she entered the nurses training, why she then took her subjects all at the hospital. And they had their teachers, they had the subjects, they had practical procedures. They were taught by Mrs. Smith. And subjects such as bacteriology, hygiene, dietetics, obstetrics, aseptic surgery, surgical techniques, material medical, and ethics for nurses were taught by the late Mrs. Baker. She was a deaconess graduate. And so then after such time as they had taken all of their courses and all then they of course prepared for their state boards just very much as they do now.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What was that ethics for teachers class?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Ethics for nurses. Ethics for nurses, excuse me. Well, she had all her books out here. It's what you don't pass on any information about a patient. And you don't talk about people to other people in any way. Or the doctors. That's right, you don't talk about the doctors.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: If they say, well now, who should I have for such and such a thing? Or do you think my doctor is alright?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They teach you how to handle these situations and answers. I have this paper here. This gives the ones that I had to take a state board on. I finally found that. Oh good. And I took anatomy, nursing sick children, dietetics, physiology, gynecology and obstetrics, urinalysis, bacteriology, material medical, that is the study of medicine. And infectious diseases, surgical nursing and medical nursing and hygiene. Oh. They split the subjects a little bit, I think, when they gave the board. Well, yes, you had these books that you studied. Yeah. But now, of course, and she tells me that they had all their classes at night. They studied on this two hours off in the afternoon, but the classes were conducted at nighttime, besides this 12 hour stint of duty with the two hours off.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And then, Pearl, how much time did you have off? Like, did you ever have a day off?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, I was going to say, we had a half a day a week if it could be managed.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's right.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I know that because I came in 1931 and I worked night and they had a half a night off. So you saved your half a night so you could have a whole night off. Yeah. It was, it was, you were, you went in there to work, it seemed like, you know. Everybody worked then. And you just, if, now like now, doesn't make any difference, I don't think, a great deal of difference what the nurses do. When her coffee break comes, somebody else comes over and takes over and she goes. Or lots of times she goes anyway. And then we didn't mention that the hospital did provide full maintenance plus an allowance. Five dollars the first year, this is per month, seven dollars per month the second year, and nine dollars per month the third year. From that, if you didn't have anyone helping to support you from home, why, out of that came your uniforms, your books, and all the clothing and everything.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you have any little extras that you required?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's the money I used that I earned before I went into training.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And did you live in the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. I did all the time while I was nursing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you have to live in hospital or were you allowed to?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I didn't have to but I wanted to because I could save money. And you had such good food and all that that you really was a very pleasant place to stay. Now, we had a big dining room downstairs which had two tables, one for a doctor and Mrs. Gritman and the nurses had a table of their own. Now, the nurses and the patients and the doctors' family, they all had the same food. They had one cooked and she cooked for everybody. And of course some of the patients couldn't have everything that the nurses could eat. But their food was just as good as what the doctor himself ate. I'm trying to get your picture here. Now, this is the picture or the dress that I wore when I was just doing the cleaning and that sort of thing. This is me right in there.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: There's two nurses. Something that was comfortable to work in. Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Would it get very warm in the summer in the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, it was surprising. The only floor that I thought got real warm was the third floor. The first floor was really cool, very cool.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What was on the first floor?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, the kitchen was there and the back hall was where the nurses who were in training and I myself. And there was another girl that did the same type of work that I was doing at that time. And there was a lavatory there. And then there was a pool hall, pool table. And a waiting room and quite a spacious hall. And two doctor's offices, two large offices. And the x -ray room. And then another treatment room. Which we had all types of heat lamps and that sort of thing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Can you think of anything else that you'd like to add?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, see in the evening, that was the billiard room they called it. The doctor and his family, the relatives were there. I suppose Pearl was down there too. Oh yes. Then that was their living room. And they called it the billiard room. Of course they had a living room upstairs too, Dr. and Mrs. Pearl.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Where was their living room upstairs, Pearl? Well, don't you know they had this huge library with all these books?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, their sleeping room was right across from the diet kitchen. From the nurses' station. Then they had a room right next to that. It was kind of a small sitting room. Then they had this huge library where they had this grand piano. Because I used to have to dust all those bookkeeping cases.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Pearl, would that be the room that I'm trying to tell you that maybe they had two women in at one time there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That was after... That was the bigger room. That was after... But that's probably the room that was the library then. Yes. Oh, now we've got our locations right. You see, after Dr. Grittin left, we put four beds in that room.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was there four in it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: In that big one. So now you know what I mean and I know what you mean. And two in the other room. So we enlarged it quite a bit that way.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's the first floor.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's the second floor. Went up these big, big stairs. See, this is an old hotel.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: These stairs were beautiful, weren't they, Pearl?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, yes. The banisters and all. Yeah, and the halls on the second floor had this wainscoting on it all around. And of course I have... I'll keep that clean, too. I was a professional cleaner.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: The upstairs didn't have wainscoting?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, upstairs didn't. Was that not...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: The downstairs hall had it. But the back hall had it also. I remember that. That was a lot of work, Ben.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So were there rooms for patients then on the second floor?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They were second and third floors. Second and third. No patients on the first floor.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And third floors where they had the wards?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. You had a big ward there. You used to put tonsils in there when I was there. I think it took four beds that we used to keep tonsils. People would come in and they would have maybe three children, bring them all three at the same time to have their tonsils out so we would use those. Many times you'd have two, even if they weren't in the same family, you know.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You'd have two or three of them.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And the mothers would be on the second floor?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, on one hall. The mothers were down one hall. The surgical patients were down another hall. Some of the medical patients were on third floor.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Where is that? Or did you show her where you can give the patient fluid?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, of course now, you know, we give edu -vena in the vein. Do you suppose Dr.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Fink got this idea when he was in Europe or did he just think this up?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I don't know. He had it when I... Well, now, you want to give fluid by drop. And then you give the method very slowly to your patient. Well, this is the way that they did it in the hospital where I trained. This is just an open container that has a core fluid. You put it in there warm and hopefully it would stay warm because the patient will expel it if it hits them cold. You know, they won't absorb it. So the way we had, this came down, this hot water bottle was on, and then this went in the back. And then you would take the patient's rectum and you would regulate it by this. I think it's 20 drops of something like that. And then if they had the gas escape, it would come up and back down into here. Well, this was most unsatisfactory, actually. So Dr. Gritman invented this, so to speak. He got these. These were a quart, I suppose.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Were they a quart or two quarts?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They were a quart. Thermos bottles. So then he had a frame made, and this, I can't draw this well, but it had three legs and you could set it like here while you were getting it hooked up. And of course this is on one of these hanger things that's by the bed. And so then to let the air out, you see, so this would come down here. Here's your air vent. This had special corks in here. Then your tubing came down here. You had the same drip method and the same rubber rectal tube and all, but you had the same, the water that was coming out of here. You could give any different kinds of solutions, but this, as it came out, it was warm. You see, it kept this same temperature that was up there, so it was a very clever thing that he devised for that.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So then you had the same water that was coming out of here.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were they giving blood at that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Just direct, by the direct method, and I don't know, you know, patient to patient.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And I don't know, did you have any blood transfusions?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, I had seen it done. But we didn't have blood banks. No. When I was there, our nurses association had people ask people to come in and be typed and so they would give blood. And they would say that they could be called upon in emergencies, and so that's the way you'd call in the donor, and it would be with a direct method.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: There was no blood storage at that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. Not here. I don't know what there was blood storage in other places. The closest, we got ours out of Boise. But this went on for quite a few years, this direct method. Because I remember we had these people, this was our project. That wasn't the direct method.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: From the person to person, that's correct?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yeah, that's right, because I know one time a bunch of us nurses went down to Troy to take blood from a whole bunch of fellows. And we sent them to Lewiston to have them typed. That was it. Oh. And then we kept that on record. And then we called for... Then you could get it. Uh huh. Well, you could get the blood from Boise, you know. But you have to go through a certain number of... But no, this was, I think that's when they were...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: When we had that project?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: As far as I remember, I had only seen one direct blood transfusion, and that was from the man to his wife. Oh, uh huh. Well, there was a man that was caught, if you remember this, he was a railroad man, and he was caught between the car and a building that they went right close to down here. And this man was crushed. And I know my husband gave blood to that man, and that was by the direct method.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Was it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Uh huh. You see, then later, why they take the blood, and then it would go from the bottle in, that would be fresh blood, though. But the way back, why it was just from patient to patient. It had to go through, it went through, it came from one patient, then it went through a saline solution. And then to the other patient. And so it wouldn't clot. Yeah.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, have we got something else?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They had quite a drug room, and he had, you can tell about the hourly medications and some, his mixture?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, if he could get a patient that was running quite a fever, why he would, he would have us make up, he had a lot of small pills that he, and you put so many pills to a certain number of ounces of water. And you dissolved them, and then you had to give one teaspoonful of this solution every hour to the patient, until their fever came down to practically normal. Sometimes it would take a couple days. And giving him medication every hour is no small thing. When you had a hundred other things to do. But he did have a lot of medications. He understood his medicine. Oh, yes. He bought him many, many medicines you would buy in gallon jars.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Would he then mix the medicines himself?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: He would mix some of them, and he would tell us how to mix some of them. Sometimes he would show us how to mix, we would mix some of them. And then he made the first one. Then if we needed any more, then we made it up. And we had a scale, how much powder to weigh out and everything. So you learned quite a bit about how to make up your medicine for your patients, and how to, you know, what type, how often you can give a certain medication, and all that sort of thing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Now is that opposed to now getting the drugs from a pharmacy?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, yes. And giving them because they're already prepared.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They're individually prepared now and given, you know. Well, the pharmacy, they, I don't think they make up too many prescriptions because everything comes made up. Here you come, now I don't know how many companies put out your antibiotics. And of course they're getting out new drugs all the time, and sometimes they don't get enough research on them. We didn't have, we didn't give people sleeping pills, we gave very little morphine, very little. We had to turn this patient and work with them until we got them to some degree comfortable. And not only that, the nausea, you know, we had that ether, you had the nausea too. It was inevitable almost that people would be nauseated from the anesthetic. And you'd have vomiting and...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I'm not quite clear where the operating room was.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It was on the third floor.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And that room was practically heated with our, what do you call our sun heat now?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Solar. The skylight. Yeah, you had your skylight. And one end of the surgery, the whole wall, was all windows. And it was frosted, so you didn't get a glare from it. And that, well, was, and it didn't have anything on, any shades outside or any place. You got the sun in there all the time. Which the sun, I mean, is certain, certain number of... There are some conditions that they feel if they are exposed to the air, which helps much, can recover. Now, they used to think. Now, they definitely know, feel, that gonorrhea germ will die if you expose it to the sunlight and to the air. This would now be the time, perhaps, to mention the maggots. Oh. You used the maggots when you were on telltale.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You know what a maggot is, don't you? Those little flies?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah. Well, I saw those used, I think, shortly after I ended training.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How were they used?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you have a patient who, this patient had an injured ankle, and it became very infectious. Well, it became infected. He was in an airplane crash, I believe it was. And you put these magnets on, and they will eat all the dead flesh away from this. It's an awful thing to see. And, of course, after they have eaten all your dead flesh, then you can take them away. They've done their job. And then we had another animal, which was, it's called a leech. And they used that to reduce blood pressure. And that leech, if we just, you know, you put it on the skin and it attaches itself on both ends, and it takes the blood out of the, you know, from the patient.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How long, when did they quit using the insects?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh. I only saw the insects used once. That's all. Now, whether they used them a long time before my time, they perhaps did. Well, they used leeches. We believe in our histories where they used leeches. You know, the kings and queens, when they got bad blood, they put the leeches on them and bleed them. Of course, I suppose they were hypertensive and that sort of thing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were they supposed to suck the bad blood and no to leave the good blood alone?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I don't know that might have been, but they did use them on the royalty. I remember reading that. And your blood changes over, you know, ever so often why you get a whole cell of new blood vessels, but not blood vessels.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Blood content, you mean your blood cells die and are replenished?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So. Can you remember what was in the surgery, the operating room, the instruments and the...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, well, of course, we had two cases there of instruments. And, of course, some of the anesthesia was kept in there, not too much, because we had storage room across the hall. Just a little across the hall, we had a small storage room to keep all of our extra supplies. So we had an operating table, a little stool for the anesthetists to sit on, and some standards to hold your pants and your instrument tray. You had a standard there which your tray fit into, and then, of course, you covered it all up with your sterile sheets and things. But the surgery wasn't too large, because we never had anybody in there except the persons who were working in there at the time. People always wanted to watch, but they didn't invariably pass out by the time they got into the first room. You see, there was one great big room first where we had all of our sterilizers and our great big sink where we scrubbed up, where we got cleaned up. And then we had an instrument sterilizer which was just boiling. It just boiled those. And we had in there two tanks. One was hot, hot sterile water, and the other one was cold sterile water. And then this other huge boiler that you could put ever so many things in if you wanted to boil them, like pans and trays and anything. If you felt your gowns needed boiling before you sent them to laundry, but they usually weren't boiled, the laundry took care of that. We had a very good service from the laundry. And you didn't tell how Dr. Gritman disposed of the man that painted in the delivery room. Oh, well, he was not in the delivery room.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: In the room?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: In the room where the mother, yeah. I was in there with him. I was giving the patient an anesthetic. And the doctor was busy. And this man, this was his first baby, and he would have to be in there to watch his delivery. So after a few words, the doctor said, okay, come in. So he went in and he stood up and kind of beheaded by his wife. And when Dr. Gritman was just about the busiest trying to deliver the lady, this fella fell right over on the patient's chest. The doctor just reached over and grabbed him and let him fall on the floor and pushed him under the bed. He had to get out because he was in my way.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And of course he took time and had to change clothes. And pretty soon the baby was born.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And pretty soon this guy woke up and he said, where am I? Where am I?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: The doctor said, you're under the bed and stay there until we get through. Oh, dear. Oh, there's a lot of things, you know, that happen. People don't pay any attention to you. And so they have to find out for themselves.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were the women that were delivering, were they given any kind of anesthesia?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, I was given the anesthetic, you see.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And what impact was that on the chloroform?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Because they didn't require very much. They don't require much chloroform when they deliver. And they're not under enough so you can ask them to push when it's time to push and that sort of thing. And just about soon as the baby is born, this is stopped. And then the placenta is delivered by itself, you know, there's no pain to that. And unless the mother needs some suturing, well, they give her a little whiff of this and that takes care of everything.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Is any of that chloroform, any of the anesthesia passed on to the baby?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Very little. She doesn't get enough of it because your babies always come out howling. Because they haven't had any for very little pre -med, the mother. That's what takes a hold of your baby, is the pre -med that the mother gets.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did many of those women, either at the home delivery or at the hospital, die in childbirth?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Let's see. You know, Joanne's mother had placenta previa and she died. Yeah, she died.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What is placenta?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I'll explain it for you. Well, you see, your baby is located in the... I should have kept some of my books out. Usually the baby is located with your head down.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And then the placenta, you know what a placenta is, don't you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It's just a big... Afterbirth, they call it. Yeah, the spongy stuff for the baby to land on when it takes its jumps up and down. Well, this placenta was not in the right spot. It was a little over where they... In fact, they called it... It wasn't complete. It turns, you know. She's damaged. So, it doesn't come when it's like that, when you have a placenta previa. It doesn't come in the sac like they usually do. They have torn away from the uterine wall and then that whole thing bleeds. And unless you've got enough from... Unless the uterus clamps down real firm and real hard, sometimes they're okay. But this one didn't. And she died. And then one lady, she delivered twins. She seemed to be okay during her pregnancy. She delivered twins. And after she delivered the twins and the placenta and everything, she went into convulsion. And she never came out of that convulsion.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Never? Just kept convulsing?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, she just became unconscious and she didn't die for about three days. What caused... Do you suppose... Well, I think it was toxemia. Very toxic. People then weren't checked like they are now. You're going to receive you every so often and he keeps abreast of these conditions. But you see, they might come in just before they were ready to deliver. I remember having seen the doctor before. He was not aware of this condition at all, you see.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: About how many children would a family have, an average -sized family, about that time? With the women? How old are you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
Rachel Foxman
words: Oh, she's just an infant, isn't she? And are you married or not?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, I'm not.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You're not married?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I don't know. My dad,

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: he was married... No, he was married twice. And my first... My mother... My mother had 13 children before she died with the 13. And then he married again and this lady had seven children. My goodness! So you see, he actually had 20 children. He's very prolific. But none of these children lived, you see. They only had maybe three or four at a time and the others would be dead because they died in infancy. Oh. And, however, my stepmother only had one still birth. She had six living children. Where my mother, she had 13 children and she had only six living children.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, is that right?

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So you see, there the mortality was over half. I've often, going through the different towns in Latah County and going through the graveyards to do research, a lot of the headstones didn't have children. Oh, oh, tremendous amount of them.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What is a factor in that?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, years ago children didn't get any vitamins. They weren't properly nourished. I think if the mother couldn't nurse them... The food that they gave them, I think that I perhaps was started in to feed to drink tea as soon as I could swallow. Well, of course they had a lot of scarlet fever and smallpox. They had a lot of diseases they didn't even know about. That's right. Now, my sister, one of my sisters had six children, one had four, one had one, and that was all. So I think the one with the six, I think that was a big family. That was plenty big. They wanted large families at that time. Oh, they didn't know how to... They didn't know what to do. They didn't know how to prevent. But there were methods around to prevent it at that time. No, not when I was born. I don't think they knew that. And in the country I was born, there was no such thing. It was a natural event. They thought, the only prevention they had that they thought was alright was if you kept nursing your baby you wouldn't get pregnant.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: They would have to go and they'd nurse them as long as they could, but that wasn't so, was it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. You see, the mother would have a baby and this baby would live maybe two or three months and die. Well, it wouldn't be any time that woman got pregnant again.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Malnutrition was a good thing. Yeah, but malnutrition, that's right. You've got that now back in some of these countries. You've got malnutrition, we're in underdeveloped countries.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You see where they always want us to give to care?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, that's the area. So, if it feels a little warm, I'll open up this door.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And what year were you taking your nurses to?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I took mine in the state of Montana at the Deaconess Hospital and I trained from 1926 to 1930. Then I came here, that's why we sort of collaborated a little bit because I knew not all the things that she knows, but I was aware of some of them.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was there much difference between what you had learned even in those short years between when you were taking your nurses training and you did a change?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I presume that in the great measure she learned more than I did in that time because she worked right with the doctor. And you see our contact, I suppose there was 30 in my class and doctors gave lectures and we had regular teachers. But she was right at the scene all of the time and I think that made a great deal of difference. I told her yesterday, we were thinking a lot about boys and dates and you know, a bunch of girls, we lived in the dormitory. And I thought to myself, her life was more serious and I know that she absorbed perhaps more that I wished later I had absorbed when I came time to use it. Well, after I left the hospital, then, that was 35, and I went to work in Dr. Armstrong's office. Well, there, I was his first assistant at all the surgeries he did. And I gave all his anesthetics for him in the office. I gave all the anesthetics for his tonsils and any other thing. He did much of his emergency work and everything in his office. And I managed the whole office. And you know, get all the sterilizing, get all the buying and all that sort of thing. So, then, and went out on all the deliveries with him, day or night.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why would some women come to the hospital and have their babies and some women would stay home?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Because they couldn't afford to come to the hospital. And then, of course, there were several women that had deliveries in their homes and were set up for the delivery and kept the mother and the baby the length of time. You see, and that was a little less expensive, too. So, then, the doctor just came and it was time to deliver the baby.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Would you say that most of the births in this area were assisted by a doctor at the time that you were nursing?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think most of them were. I was caught with a couple myself.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you manage all right?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Fine. This lady, especially this one, she had had three children. And Dr. Armstrong was out there and he examined her. And he said, what he did, he said, now he can't squeak or beat me. He ruptured the sack. Well, you know, when you've had three children, just one right after the other, and you get a fourth one, and you rupture the sack, you better hang around yourself. You better not leave. Well, he ruptured the sack and he said, I'm going to town for a few minutes and I'm going to the office or someplace and you call me when you need me. Well, he hadn't even gotten to town when I was busy delivering this baby. And there wasn't anybody there to do any telephoning for me. I was there all alone. There was no neighbor or nothing. And this was a great big baby with a cord around his neck twice. Oh my goodness. Well, I managed, but the woman didn't get any anesthetic. She... I tried, yeah, I tried to, you know, stretch myself. The baby's way out, but with the cord around the neck, you couldn't, you didn't have any choice. I'd have to use both hands. And she delivered okay. And there was a little, you know, quite a little bit of noise when that great big baby came out. But,

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: and the other one was quite a normal delivery. We used to go out and stay all day with the doctor in the home.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Stay all day?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Practically. We waited for hours. All night.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You went out and lots of people didn't know when to call you. You know, they had no idea. So, doctor, he would examine the patient and he would leave a nurse there and call me when you're ready. Well, you'd be there all day. And then maybe 10 o 'clock at night that person would deliver. And, boy, the next day you really had it. And then you perhaps had to help do a tonsil or two or something like that, you know. But I enjoyed that work very much, the office work. Because in a surgery you really can't see what's being done unless you're right there helping.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I never could see very much by standing, you know. And so you see, you asked me if I felt like I was much. She had such a diversified training where in the larger hospital you were in OB for six months, surgery for six months. And so then you didn't have. Some nurses come out of training of fairly large hospitals and would not have seen a certain type of delivery. Like, we had girls come down from Canada and help us. You know, come to the hospital just to help. And one night I was still taking care of an OB case and the doctor, he was around, but he would be in and out of the hospital. And this nurse stayed way after it was time for her to go off duty. And I asked her, I said, you don't have to stay because I've done this alone many times. And I said, you come. And she didn't really say anything and she kept staying there. Pretty soon I was ready for the doctor and we set up. And we delivered a breach. That's when the feet come first, or the buttocks. I guess breach is buttocks and footling is feet. And when I got all through and we put the patient back to bed, she stayed and helped us put the patient back to bed and everything. And then she told me, she said, you know, I have never seen a breach delivery and that's the reason I wanted to stay and watch. So, and she trained in a large hospital in Calgary, Canada. She'd never seen it. So I suppose she had six months. I suppose, and she stayed there for a length of time in there. Yes, and it just didn't happen while she was on duty. That's right, so you didn't see it. Oh, and here I was on duty practically, well I was on duty all the time with Dr. Armstrong when I was working in his office.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How large was your class at Gritman?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, the class I was in was only three. So many have dropped out.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And how many were in your class?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Thirty. Thirty, that is the difference.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You see, it's all together different, different sort of thing. Well, I think, too, when you have a doctor who is very anxious to put out good nurses. Yes, and then you had a personal relationship. And he told you all these things where the doctors could have cared less if I learned anything or not.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Don't you know?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's right.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: What about the class?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: If he had something and he knew the nurse on the floor would have, could take time to come up and look in the surgery. He sent for them. He had them come up so they could observe that, whatever unusual thing he was doing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How old were you when you came to Moscow?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, now I'm not going to tell you that. Oh, okay. I can tell you how old I was.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How old were you when you came?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Twenty -three. Twenty -three. And you had graduated from high school, I mean. And gone to nurses training. Just out of high school. Right out of high school. I saw my first baby delivered when I probably was eighteen when I saw my first one. Because I was almost eighteen when I went in training. They wouldn't take you until you were very near. And so, I went in August, I think, and I was eighteen in September. But, oh, I was so thrilled. I was just so thrilled when I saw that baby. We were just observing when I saw this baby born. Well, the first one I saw born, it just, um, somebody, somebody had the baby down there. And then whoever was giving you an anesthetic was trying to push, to bring the mother up in bed. And they were trying to push it. Well, Dr. Ritten wasn't aware of what that person was doing because he wasn't quite through with the baby. And it was a heavy baby. And as she pulled this patient up in bed, why, that severed the cord. Oh, that was nice. And so, you know, there were the, blood would be flying in all directions. And of course he grabbed the instrument and clamped it right away. And neither one of them lost enough blood to do any harm as far as that was. But it just scared me. Like we used to have a, we always had a student living at the hospital doing various things, you know. And sometimes some of them would say, that just scared me to hell.
