And when I saw this, why, it just scared me to hell. And it scared you to hell. You haven't told anything about Dr. Armstrong and his jokes. Well, I don't remember. I can't remember. Dr. Marinol wanted her to tell Dr. Armstrong had a weird sense of humor. He wanted her to tell something about that. I can't remember of any jokes. Of course, he was always playing jokes on us girls in the office. We had a secretary and myself. There was just two of us in the office. And I came there in 35 and she came in April of 36. But the only thing I can remember doing, he always would pile something on top of the door when we came in on April Fool's Day. That would fall on us. Sometimes water, sometimes just a box of bunches or anything that. But I think he told his stories, not so much to us as he did to his patients. Perhaps. Because I remember we would have to go in, into his private office and tell him that there were other patients out there to be seen. Yes, he always took a great deal of time. And he did a good deal of hunting and fishing in his office. Well, you know, in a way, he should, along with his practice, he should have taken psychology. Because he did more by talking to people than actually doing something for them. When they were just sick, you know, and didn't have to be operated or something of that sort.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: My experience about going out to Viola, Dr. Armstrong, there wasn't enough work that kept me busy at the hospital. I think I applied and they just took me, you know, if they needed me for a day or so. Anyway, this man had come for Thanksgiving dinner to some friends and while he was there he got pneumonia. He was merely a friend. He was the man who came to dinner and got pneumonia. Well, so he was very ill and Dr. Armstrong took me out there to special him. When you specialed, you worked 24 hours a day. You could have a little time off in the afternoon. But you worked right around the clock. You looked after this patient. And at that time they put a patient in a very cold room, which was uncomfortable for you to try to manage to do for them. And then you would dash in and dash out and just freeze, you know. The patient was very uncooperative. He coughed and spit all over. Anyway, I could have a little cough if there was such. So the people didn't have any and they got an old bed spring and they put it on four pieces of stove wood about the right height. And in the night I was dozing off and the whole thing fell down with me. And when I told him, he just thought that was the craziest thing he did. He just enjoyed anything that happened to you. He loved it. And these people had a boy that was nine years old. So that after noon I could go out and the lady of the house watched the patient. And I could go out maybe for a couple of hours. The first day, I went first time, we skated on a little pond. And then another day he had a pony and I rode the pony and the pony dumped me right in the manure pile. But you were really too tired to almost want to get out and into the air or anything. And the doctor left me out there for two weeks and finally came and pronounced the man better and took me home again. But one of his tricks was the snow was on the ground and he was a tall man and he would step as far as he could and then he'd look over his shoulder. You couldn't possibly step in his footsteps. That just amused him. And he'd say, no, and here you are struggling and waggling along in the snow and he's just laughing at you. But these people and this man, he stayed all this time and he didn't want to pay for all this. He hadn't ordered this nurse and whatnot. I was ages and ages getting my money. And I think at that time, I believe it was six dollars you got for this 24 hour stint. Besides, of course, the people whose home he was at, why they had to feed me and that sort of thing. We had to take the bedpan out to the old pump, out and back, to empty the bedpan and wash it out and take care of it.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So when it didn't freeze on you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, that was home nursing. This is the pictures that are the uniform that you wore after you graduated. Well, we wore that same type of uniform except the dress was blue, the apron was white.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, were your sleeves long?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. Our sleeves were short and the dress was striped. No, we always had long sleeves.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And it's the cap that shows where you went to school?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes, my cap has a black band on it right down around the very rim. It goes up about that much on the rim and that shows where you go. And also, your pin.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you show her your pin?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You get your pin when you graduate. It's right there on my apron. But you had very pretty pins.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Do you get capped and then you get a pin?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You get capped in three months after your probationary, period. Then, if you pass that successfully, and they feel that you're the right temperament and so on, why then you are capped. At our place they had a capping ceremony and that's quite an event in your life. You're pretty thrilled because you don't know what's going to happen to you up until it's time. So then when you graduate, why then you get the black band on your cap. Very coveted black band.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Is that on all nurses' caps?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, that was on the deaconess cap. But you will find on other caps there will be black bands. Maybe they'll be in a little different place or a little wider. But there is a difference. But now you have a choice. You can wear that. You've seen that little ruffled cap they wear. A little organdy cap. Kind of a dainty little thing. Well, they do at my training school. They could have their choice. They could wear the original deaconess cap or they could wear those. And I noticed a lot of the nurses wore the little frilly caps.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So it would be the cap within three months and then upon graduation something like that?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: The black band. And the pin. And the pin. And the hospital gave you your pin.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What got you interested in nursing?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I went to...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: what would you call it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I'm sorry, the seminar or something. And this woman got up. It was over at Bozeman, Montana. And I went to school at St. Ignatius, Montana. And there was about four of us that went over to Bozeman. And this woman got up and she told us what would be open to women.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: What should a woman take?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: We were seniors.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: What should you plan to do with your life?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I had never really thought about much what I would do. And she was very fluent and made a very rosy picture and how you would be very dedicated and it would be a very satisfying life. So just in that instant I decided I'd be a nurse and my mother was so against it. She thought she was the old -fashioned type. You must not handle the men and do for the men. So, but my father thought this was fine. This was it. So I could go. And then I had a doctor. We had a doctor friend that you had to have somebody to vouch that you were a good character and this and that. So then I proceeded.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Can you remember what the other choices they said you could have other than nursing?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, you could teach. You could, I think teaching seemed to be foremost. But the fact that they said nursing, you didn't have to have the money. You got your board and room at this time and you were really working for your education in nursing school. They just gave you, I got a very similar amount of money per month as Pearl did. And your folks, my folks were able to, you went there with, I don't know, six uniforms and all these big aprons. Your folks furnished that and it was all sewed. Then after that they furnished your clothes. But this appealed to me because I could make my own, almost my own way. Let's see, teaching, I never cared for teaching so I didn't even consider the teaching. And then I had another friend who was very interested and at that time I thought she would come down to Spokane and go in with me. And I think that influenced me somewhat too, but she didn't.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was there a big push when it was time for you to decide what you were going to do? To say, either you were going to do this or you were going to get married?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, I never thought, I came from a small town and I would see these girls get married and have a whole batch of children. And this seemed to me like it was the end of life. And I did not want to do this. Same here. This was not, this just was not for me. It didn't seem a great decision but always, now this is what my mother would want for me but this wasn't what my father wanted for me. I never wanted to get married and wash out some dirty socks all the time. Of course, as you, I don't know if you, I didn't even consider or was never serious about, you know, some girls at 18 are very serious about marriage but no, I wasn't. I never got serious. You missed the boat entirely. But I was married when I came to Moscow.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And you never married?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But she delivered. Oh, I want to tell this about you, Pearl. I like to be independent. The people, the women and everybody that Pearl took care of, Pearl was the angel in life, I can tell you that. And they thought that their babies could not be born if Pearl wasn't there. And as you came in these stairs, in this front door, Pearl usually was up at the top of those stairs. And as I can remember, she helped deliver my child and I thought that I cannot have a baby unless Pearl is there. Well, thank you. And so I, her name is McLeod, so I named my son Gerald Mack for her. But I suppose that Pearl has helped deliver many, many babies.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I wish I'd kept track of the babies I helped deliver.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Have you got an idea of how many it might be?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I have no idea.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: A hundred thousand?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, all the years I've been, especially the years, but then after Dr. Armstrong died in 1950, I think it was 1957, see then we had different doctors in the office, then I didn't go out on deliveries. And even before he died for some time, he wouldn't take any deliveries outside the hospital. Well, then we had a more sophisticated delivery room and all this at the hospital by that time, so that people felt real safe and secure and most everybody was coming to the hospital. Now they've had a return again to home deliveries, you know. Oh, it just, so it seems like it goes in cycles.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But I have done, you know, well, being there all the time in the hospital with all the sick people all the time, you had to be with somebody who was sick.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes, you couldn't escape it, could you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You couldn't escape it. And I would, I worked nights for four years, and so then I could watch the patient up to the point where it was time to get Pearl and the doctor, you see, because I had other people to look after. And so then Pearl and the doctor would come and then they would do the delivery.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So your major work was in obstetrics?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, obstetrics and surgery, I would say. You always bathed the babies too. Oh yes, yes, I bathed the babies. You did everything about them. But lots of times, if, you know, the other girls had to, they had to get their share in too. But I would have, you know, lots of mornings, I would be taking care of three or four mothers, giving their baths and things like that. And somebody else would be bathing the babies. And so that way you got things done. But I meant at the time, during the nights you always came. Oh yes, during the four deliveries. Before the deliveries you came during the time I worked there, because you couldn't do a delivery and take a lot of time. You couldn't take care of your operatives or whatever else was going on. You know, you can watch them up to a point, but you don't. I was always awfully glad when it was time to have her come.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You worked in deliveries then?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, no, I didn't work in delivery. Only just what I did in training. I just have done really general duty. I say that her experiences are so much, so much more than mine. Because in all fields, you see, I did just general duty, surgical and medical. I think I preferred surgery though. I think I like surgery better. But not surgery room, taking care of surgicals and medicals.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why do you say you preferred surgery?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, I just like the better. I like to see the inside of people. And surgical patients are much more interesting than medical patients in my... I like to see the advance, the recovery period. There's a challenge there, I think. And it just goes, you know, on a plane. There's the bad days and then all of a sudden here, and then recently they're going home. I mean, an uneventful. There's a challenge in making people comfortable.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What's the difference between medical and surgical?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, for instance, if you had a rheumatic or some anemia or any of the things that have not required a surgical procedure. Of course, when I was nursing, we still had typhoid patients.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I had scarlet fever down there, you remember that?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. Was it epidemics or was it just... No, I had it when I was there. And we never had an epidemic of scarlet fever, not scarlet fever, I mean typhoids. But we did have typhoid patients to take care of in the hospital. Now, didn't Dr.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Armstrong's daughter have scarlet fever too, when she was a little girl?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I don't know if she was, she took care of her at home. Yes, I think she had it, because I think he told me that, and I never knew where I got it. No. See, I was grown. And Miss Wove had scarlet fever too. We had her isolate. Now, I had smallpox when I was in nurses training. And I don't mean smallpox, I mean typhoid fever. They never knew the help. People just asked me where I'd been on a picnic, where I'd been drinking water, everywhere I'd been, and they could not find out. No, about 1927. Your medical nursing is with your sick people who come in. Anything, other than surgery. Just anything that doesn't require surgery. Headache, anything other than surgery.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was there certain fear, as a nurse going in and nursing people that possibly had something communicable?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You don't have...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Not much infection?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, well you take very, very good, you've been taught how to handle infections. You handle people with syphilis, you handle people with gonorrhea, but you know how to handle them and not contact it yourself. I think one of the first patients I ever had had gonorrhea.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And of course, when our typhoid patients, we always had to put on gowns and caps. That's isolation. Yes, before we went in to do anything for them.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I wanted to ask you about the flu epidemic. You were in Moscow during that time period. Yeah, that's the time I came.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What was the situation like at that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I'll tell you, everybody was sick.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And I had been at home down there, and there was flu down there. But, and a lot of people, many more people died down there.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Then did here?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Then they did here. Now I don't know why, but maybe this place had more doctors. I don't really know who the doctors were when I came here. I know Dr. Griffin was here, Dr. Clark was here.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And there was a Dr. Stevenson here, I think. But I really don't know how many doctors were here when I came. But I know Dr. Griffin was on his feet, sometimes 24 hours a day. And as I said, in making these country calls, he had a driver. And he would sleep until they would get to the place where they had, where the sick person was.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: He wasn't going in an automobile by that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, in the fall of the year, he perhaps had a car by that time. He had one of the first cars in Moscow, I've been told. Yes. But at the fall of the year here, it isn't safe to just start out in a car. And you don't know when you're going to get there. You know that you didn't have the roads that you have now.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It was mud.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. And only a teen could get there. And I have been told that he did ride a bicycle, too. When he first came here. I suppose he did. On little short calls and stuff. I imagine he had any type of... Conveyance, every type of conveyance. I think that I've been told that his car was red.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Have you ever been told that? The red Ford?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. The only car I remember, the first car I remember, we... I'm not sure. In a way, he took us out, took several of us gals and Mrs. Pippen out for a ride. And this lady came in, in labor. That's a great thing. And we got a flat. Oh no. And somebody from town came out and took the doc and brought him in. And we were out there until somebody could come out and fix the flat. Now the barn was still there when, in 1930. Yeah, it was... I don't know. It had lots of old magazines and stuff. And I remember that part. But it wasn't used for horses or anything. I don't know when they got rid of the team or when they got rid of the cows. I don't know exactly. I don't know either, but... But they did have the cows and the horses when I came.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How far out would he go to make a home call?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, they would go miles. Yes, they'd go clear out past Viola. Oh, Dr. Armstrong and I went to Bovial. We started out sometime during the night to deliver a baby. And of course they had logging accidents, lumbering accidents and that sort of thing. You couldn't fly them out with the copter like they do now, you know. But that's the furthest I had gone for delivery was up to Bovial. But their delivery... Oh, I know I went with Dr. Gritman on some type of emergency. And it seems like we went way beyond Troy for miles and miles. And up into Fernwood or wherever, you know. Of course I'm not very sure to remember all those places. But there weren't much of a place. There wasn't much of a place. And people would be ill and they wouldn't have... They got along somehow without the doctors, if they could. Oh, yes, sure. They only called them when they were really sick. No, I...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Isn't that a pretty pin? What's this animal?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's a beaver.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: A beaver?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: The untiring worker. That was I.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Mine was about the like the inside of that. And then mine has FJS in it. And it means for Jesus' sake in the middle of my pin. Was Deaconess started as a... It's a deaconess, Methodist. No, Methodist.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Is it Methodist?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Deaconess Hospital is a Methodist institution. You know, the deaconess, those women, used to have various... Oh, one was the treasurer and all that. But now it's all a bit done by a business manager. This is what I wore when I was working in the office.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: How was it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: For Dr. Maranow. I've lost three pins. And I think I'd lose them going to the laundry. Now the hospital had smaller pins than that. And they had some type of blue on it. But they still had the beaver on it. Oh. And one nurse sent her pin back here to the hospital for them to have. And it was a small pin. And she also sent a note. And Boyd let me read it. But I didn't know that girl at all.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I didn't know her.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now this mine is just about the size of the inside of that. And then this initials in there, the initials are cut out in there. And it's not as elaborate. You wouldn't notice my pin like you would this one. I'm going to look and see. I think I have Mrs. Gritman's pin. If I have it, I'll give it to the hospital. And also a cap. We displayed all of our different caps because over the years, then you see we get a good many nurses who have come and gone and that sort of thing. So one time we collected a cap. Yes, we had all. And you were in on that pearl. We all did our caps up. It was kind of interesting. It would be nice if they would do it again. We displayed at David's store. We got quite a few caps there. It would take an awful lot of space now. I wouldn't even bother with it now. It was real interesting to people as I remember when we had them in there. Because they come from all over the country.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: I don't mean to imply that nursing is an honorable profession in itself, but what would have been the chances if you wanted to go on to be a doctor? Was there a desire to do that or would it have been hard for women to become doctors?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think it was harder years ago for a doctor. In fact, it wasn't the thing to do for a woman to be a doctor. Although we had Dr. Mary Rodney and Spokane. When I went training Dr. Mary Rodney, they were very active. When I was still working at the hospital, we had a Dr. Gilchrist. The lady, Dr. Woo, was very good. Was there a Dr.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Wheeler here too?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Dr. Wheeler was Spokane. That was one of my... He used to swear. He was kind of like Dr. Armstrong though. He liked to pull things on. Dr. Wheeler is dead now. We really did get to know him because he was always pulling little jokes and things. He was a good surgeon. He could take an appendix out in an awful short time. I don't know how many doctors there were at the Deaconess when I was there. I helped a doctor from Tacoma who was here. He was taking Dr. Armstrong's place. Well, this doctor from Tacoma wanted to get a divorce from his wife so he had to have it in Idaho. He established a residence in Idaho for six months. So while he was hanging around here, Dr. Armstrong decided to go someplace. I don't know where he went. So I helped this fellow take out an appendix. He took it out in ten minutes. I wouldn't want him to take mine out. Oh, that's the way Dr. Wheeler was. He'd be so proud, you know. And then pretty soon, two or three days, they were going back to surgery again. Yeah. Dr. Grippen worked... I wouldn't say that he was a real fast operator. But yet it didn't seem any time that he got his work done. He knew just... He didn't have to hunt around for anything. He knew where it was located. And he said... Now, I would hear him tell some of the doctors that would come in there. And he'd say, now, you know where the appendix is located. You don't have to open up the whole thing in order to... Some of them do, though. Some do.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That happened to you, didn't it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. And he said, all you have to do is make your incision big enough and take a forcep and go down there and pick up that appendix. And he said, now, you don't have to take your pocket and put it inside out in order to see what you've got. He said, you can put your hand in your pocket and feel and you know what you've got. So he would tell these fellows this. So... Well, now, when Dr.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Gritman went abroad to England and to Germany, was his big interest surgical procedures then?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think so.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And then, Meos. He went back to Meos several times. Because they were personal friends with Meos. Now, where did Dr. Gritman take his... In Cincinnati, Ohio, I believe it was. I was quite sure. That was... And then he came back to Dayton, or Dayton, Washington. And he worked in a doctor's office for a while.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, Cincinnati College of Medicine and Surgery.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did he take his degree?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, it doesn't say. I suppose no one actually... I imagine. Well, it says he came to Moscow directly. But he worked, I know that he worked for this doctor in Dayton for a while.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Now, was Carithers Hospital in operation when you came? When I came?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think it was. I'm quite sure it was.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Also a private hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It was a private hospital.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did it handle different cases than Gritman Hospital did?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Not that I know of.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Pearl was... Well, I don't know if you know this either.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Was it built for a hospital, or had it been an apartment house, or what had it been?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I don't think any of them were built for a hospital.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, let me see. I just...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Is there a picture of the whole hospital in here?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, there isn't. And I have no idea how to go about it to get my picture. I had a picture of the hospital before there was any remodeling done on it. Because they remodeled some on it after I came.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But I... I wasn't there the day they took the picture. Oh, yes. Crithers Hospital was a city club.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, was it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yep. It was a city club. And you see upstairs, they have these... They had these little rooms. There was a great big space in the center. Great big floor. And that was their dance floor. Then there were all these little rooms around. And those were the powder rooms. And then down... Now, they had their surgery up on third floor. On second floor. They didn't have any third floor. The first and second. I never did look... I was in there, you know, to see somebody, I suppose, sometime or other. I never did see around the hospital. Now, they paid... During the depression time, they paid a dollar a day for a nurse.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Dollar a day?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: For 12 -hour duty.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It was 12 hours. It was a 12 -hour person on an 8 -hour day.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, we didn't have an 8 -hour day.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Never heard of such a thing for a long time, did we?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you had. You had these two hours off in that 12 -hour time. So it was...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And they have never returned it. That's too bad.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, you perhaps listened to your tape and threw half of them out. I didn't throw it out, yeah. And you may want to come back. You may want to think of things that you might want to come back and...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I did want to ask you just a little bit more about Carruthers Hospital if you hadn't heard of that.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I was wondering if there was any other person that I could think of that would know more. Well, now I'll tell you one would be Abe Goffin is well versed on these things. We talked to him. And he perhaps knows about it as much as... Because I never put foot in that house until it was a rooming house. I see. I know nothing about the hospital facilities.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Was it, but it was a hospital when you came here.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: It may have been, I don't know for sure. But then I could ask Mrs. Henson about, or you could ask her about the career there. She was a good friend of Mrs. Carruthers. Well, you can ask her because... And she may have told her things about it. Of the second Mrs.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Carruthers?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Uh huh. Well, you see, then it was Dr. McGee's when I came here. And I, as I say, I just really had seen inside the door there. Nora worked there, you know. Oh.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did they have an ambulance service very early here from the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. Sure. The artillery took care of it for many, many years. Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Just when they started to be automobiles or when they were still using horse and wagon?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, when I came here, when a patient died, they came with a horse and a hearse, you know. That's what they had when I first came here. Now, whether they took the live people in there too or not, I don't know. Of whether something... Because Short didn't have the, have the, until 1922, Short bought that establishment. And then it was down on 1st and Jackson. Not 1st and Jackson, 3rd and Jackson, where they, uh, uh, Dendel, where they medical...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Uh, Medical Arts Building is.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But then and when I was, they always had their horses in their hearse with the French... Well, Short's operated it, and it was not a, it was just a community service is what it was. It didn't pay in any sense of the word. It was... When Short had the sense of... Oh, the ambulance service. No, they didn't want it. Uh huh. Well, but they had to take it in order to for a while, I think. Oh, they had it for quite a few years. Yes.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But, uh...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And you said that what brought you to Moscow originally was that you were going to be able to get extra money by coming down here to work?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Mm hm.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I could get $5 a month more up here than I could at, at Colfax, where I had started. Which was also a hospital. Yes. It was St. Ignatius Hospital over there. I don't know how large it there. But I, there I had, didn't have much to do except do the trays and do some of the dishes and set up the trays. I was just worked in the diet kitchen, that's all. I didn't get out.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, then did you live in the, did you have room in there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They had a, they had kind of a dorm that three of us girls lived in. Oh. Uh huh. And of course it's a Catholic hospital. And one of the gals had been there, no doubt, quite a while. And, uh, he was, he was a Catholic. And she was so nice to me to come and she said, now you're going to work on second place. And there's this, Sister Rosalie, and she is a devil. Well. Be careful of her. So I kind of, I got kind of afraid. But she was real nice to me. Not at all, what this gal. Because the people that were the hardest on you were the ones you got the best instruction and training from. Well, yeah, but I didn't, you know. You know, I stayed there. And Sister Rosalie wasn't the only one. Sister Alge's helped, helped me too. Or told me what to do and when. And so she wasn't the only one. But the person, now I had never seen a Catholic priest.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And I had to go down and see Sister Superior because you didn't work there unless Sister Superior interviewed you.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And I went down, and there you go down this dark hall, and who would I meet but a priest?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Didn't know how to handle that. I was just shivering in my boots. But he went this way and I went my way. We didn't stop to chat or anything.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So how long were you there?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I was just there one summer.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And you just got it as a job or were you particularly interested in working at a hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, I just got it as a job and I had a bill there I had to work out.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Is that your appendix?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: My appendix.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How much was the appendix at that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: $150. That was a lot of money.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did that take care of the operation and the room?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. The room, the doctors had nothing to do with the hospital except work there. My room, I was in a, I think a three bed ward and it was $2 a day.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: About how much is it now a day in a hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, it's the 80 something maybe 90 now down here. It's close to $100. It's very close to $100. When they charge you with medicine, medication and stuff. Oh, you get a lot of extra. There's a lot of extra too. It goes over $100. Yeah, it runs over $100. And when I started in training, I think our, which were all private rooms, our maternity rooms, it seemed to me that they were $5 or $6.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And of course our patients didn't get bathed every day. Like they pretend here. Now when we gave a patient a bath, we didn't ask that patient to do any part of that bath at all. And now here they think they're giving you a bath every day, but they come in, they wash your back and say, now you can finish the rest of you and I'll be back a certain time. Sometimes it's two hours before they come back. I don't know whether you've ever been in the hospital or not. We always finished everybody we started. We finished them up. We finished them up. If you started the back, you finished it. And some of the medical patients who weren't in bed all the time, they didn't get a bath that often, you know. And my aunt, they weren't paying more than about two and a half or three dollars a day either. And then we passed the wash bowl of water and set off the things to brush your teeth and your wash cloth and everything was right out there. And you got to wash your face and your hands real good before you ate. Yeah, that's right. And now they toss a little thing at you, a little piece of paper thing and there was some stuff on the wet on it, some disinfectant. And lots of times, you know, they'll bring your tray to you and they won't even ask you if you have had your face washed or had your hands washed. And some, I would always tell them, I said, now I haven't had any wash cloth or anything and I'm not ready for breakfast. So then they get a little thing and they say, well, I'm not ready for breakfast. I'm going to get a washcloth and give it to me. Some places they will give you a warm, a warm wet washcloth. Uh -huh, yes.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: We used to just really have a good platter, didn't we?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, yes. We had to pick those all up and clean them all out and put them all back in the bedside table before breakfast. Yeah, and then after breakfast, a little while after breakfast, you'd have to get it all out and give the patient a bath. We had so much more to do with eating breakfast. We had to give each patient a bath. Oh, yes. You really, you know, in a way, when I would give a patient a bath, I would get their whole history. Oh, yes, and if you don't, and that's something which has definitely been lost. And even now in the later years when I worked at Gritman, it was the maid who cleaned the room that would come to me and say, Mrs. Smith has a problem.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Will you go and talk to her?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Because she had talked to the cleaning lady, and I had never had that much to do with her that I knew that she had a problem. But, you know, they'd come and say, no, she's worried about the children, or she's worried about something the doctor said, and then you'd go and talk to her.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But if you bathe the patient, as Pearl said, you get the whole history, don't you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Sure, if you want. And you feel like, and they feel like they're your friend, and they will confide in you. But when, this hospital, when Dr. Gritman had it, a patient wasn't a number, they were the person, you called them by Mrs. So and Mr. So, you didn't say... Number 28. Yeah, you didn't say number 28, or you didn't go around and call everybody by their first name. Children was okay, but not any adult. They were all Mr. So and So.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I would think that now because the patient is a patient, they're not going to be able to get the care they need. The town is so much larger, and probably a lot more people now are using Gritman than they did when you were saying.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, yes, sure, that's, yes, I realize that. But it still doesn't mean that you should keep it. That still doesn't mean that...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: You don't have a nurse -patient contact, do you? Really, really?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now you can take your nurses, you have their BBS degree or whatever other degrees they have. Well, a sick patient who comes in there, and they're going to be able to get the care they need, they don't give a damn what she has in that field, because it's the person who takes care of that patient who the patient looks to for help. And not the great big executive gal who comes around and... Says, good morning, I'm Mrs. So and So.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And who cares?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's the last you see of her. You don't see any of her. The last time I was down there, I was in the hospital. I was down here. Now, I suppose they prompt, they brag on how their surgical, no, medical nurses, when they dispense their medicine, how they are on time, and nobody else gets to handle a pill. Well, I was supposed to have this medicine four times a day. I was supposed to have it on my noon tray, and it was 2 .30, and I had not had my medicine. So I called somebody, and they went by, and they said, well, they came in the room, of course. They said, well, they would see about it. She went, and she never came back. And I called again, and a second person came. She would do the same thing. She'd go and see about it. And she left and never came back. And the third person left and never came back. I suppose that's the last thing you would do.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: How about passing the buck?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I don't know what happened to them, whether they stepped in a hole or what.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Anyway, then, a nurse that I know, who is just a practical nurse and is an older person, and I said, for heaven's sake, don't they trust you with a pill around here?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I said, I haven't had my pill, and it's very important that I have it. And she said, I'll go out and see what can be done. And she goes out there and comes back with a pill. With the pill. They let her bring it. Yeah. And that pill nurse, that day, I never did see her. Of course, they have an awful lot of meetings that you had to stop. That bothered me at the last, was you had to go, and, well, we'll say you went at 1 .30 to 2 .30. It took the time right out of the time when you were making your rounds, getting your medication comfortable for the next shift and everything. And then you went to this meeting, and that was, of course, more important. And they are important, but they had so many of them that you never felt you had taken the proper care, if you were of the old school, that you'd taken the proper care of these people, because you had... But they do need, they do need to have this education. Of course, things have changed. They have changed so much, and you have so much paperwork, so nobody can... They've changed the charting again now. They have. I heard that at the last meeting. Well, I heard that. And the doctors didn't care much for it. They asked if they wouldn't bear with it for a while and see if they would become accustomed to it and why they could.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Are you still nursing?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, I don't. Nursing more, but I go to the board of directors meeting. Well, now, you know, once they do that down here, to a certain extent, one person comes along, takes your temperature, and then another one comes along... Washes your back, and then maybe she comes back, and maybe she doesn't, maybe somebody else finishes you. And there's a nurse, she graduated from Innsbruck, Anson Place, and she said she went down into Oregon, and someplace in the middle of the state, she went to the hospital, and she said it was very important to have nurses in Washington and especially in Oregon. They had tried out both ways, giving you a full command of one patient, or... And they said that one hospital had done that, and they had gone back as to giving you, the nurse, a certain number of patients and let her do everything. They liked that much better. Much better, in my mind, it's much better. because now at the last when I worked down here, well you had, you were team nursing, and you see, for instance, I was a team leader, and then I had so many people under me and so many patients. Well then I would just sign these people that were under me to look after these people, but I didn't have this personal contact that I liked because I had paperwork and paperwork and meetings and stuff, you see. And this, I know quite a few hospitals did this. They went back. And I suppose you weren't real sure whether the people were getting all the attention. I didn't know. And lots of times, you know, I would get in, and you can't get away from what you've been taught when you're young. You just cannot get away from it. So you just would get in and take care of people. If you just had the time, you would get in there and fix them up yourself because then you'd know. But some of them really are, you know, need more attention than others, and you just feel like you've neglected them. And some of your aides who couldn't care less, you get a different variety of them every day, and some of them are very, very good.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: They're very good. It just sounds like the approach in the hospitals has changed so much, like the one you started. Oh, heavens.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I'm sure it has. I'm sure it has. Yes. But then... There's no comparison because, well, your formal education, of course, you have more of that, but they don't have, we had practical appliance of what we learned. Yeah, some, you take a nurse, if she's not practical, she's no good. We have nurses, too, that come out of the two -year courses that they don't know how to catheterize and do those things. They learn it after they get out, you see, from the ones who have already gone the road. But we learned all that before we ever got out. But...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Now, is that because technology wasn't as advanced?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's right.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And you were there since when?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That's right.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think the patients got the care they needed at that time of the year, or the century. Yes, I think they did. But, as I say, the formal education, they have much more formal education than what we ever had, but we have practical. Practical knowledge. In our opinion, perhaps, I think that the practical nurse of today, not the practical, the LPNs, had about what I had when I was in nurses' training. Mm -hmm. I think their education, what you could call it, just about the same. I know one of the nurses out here at the Good Samaritan who had a two -year training. And she said, many, many times have I wished that I had taken three years training. She says, I feel I have missed so much in just two years. Of course, now you can go on. You can go on and then get your BS degree. Oh, yes. You don't have to stop with the two years because you get credit, and you can just go right along.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did they have a policy of making you go back and take classes once you had graduated as a nurse?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Not when we graduated. No. We did go to, finally we - We went to lectures and things, some up here at the university.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And then the doctors, some of the doctors, when they came in, knew, but they soon got tired of that, didn't they?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And didn't respond when we'd like a lecture on this or that. I remember when Dr. Valder came here, he was very good about giving us lectures. And he was the one, they used the intravenous, he used the intravenous so much. So he taught us about electrolyte balance and that sort of thing, you know. But we had never, of course, had any of that before. So you do, if the doctors are willing to lecture now, you do. And then we had various times at the university.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: You were lectured then when you were taking classes, when you were taking your nursing training, there at the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: In the evenings?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, sometimes in the evenings. And Dr. Gribman would - Send for you. Yeah, anytime he wanted you to know something was going on in surgery, he thought you should see, he would send for you. And many times he was doing things, he was telling you all along, you know, what your part in this would be.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now I - And now, see, now the nurses went out to Whitworth from the deaconess. And now they didn't do that, you see. When I was in training, your lectures all came from, you had your teacher that gave you all - Materia medical, and then you had one for bacteriology, we had more teachers. But we had a great many doctors, like, but they were all in the evening.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Is there anything that you had written down that I didn't tell you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, we kind of garbled it up, but - Tell me just what it is.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think it's - Is that about it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I think that's about it. The only thing, I went back to the, now you don't need to have your thing on, but I have to tell you, it was my little story, wasn't it, about the man, every probationer learns to give the man a urinal.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And so, but this little probationer, she, she was pretty shy, and so the man said, could he have a vase? And she wondered, well, she looked around, and she didn't know just what, you know, he meant, and so finally she said to him, well, mister, how big is your lily?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, they were telling this same old story. This, you see, the deaconess has all changed since I was there, you know, this nice modern place, and the one nurse, a friend of mine, and she said, well, I'm sure that the only thing that is the same, when you were here and now, is the same old story they still tell about the probationer, and they were telling this when I was there. Well, I had a nurse who was a patient at the hospital. Now this is kind of a half -color story, and I shouldn't even tell it to you girls, because I don't think. Because you're so young. Yes, you're so young compared to, just think this little 24 -year -old girl. Yeah, all right. And she was evidently working in one of these small lumbering camps for...

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Company hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Company hospital, lumber camp, that's right, you know. And the doctor was out, he seemed to be out one whole day, and that morning he had done a hernia on a patient. Now that's an operation for a rupture, you know. Well, the doctor didn't come, and these girls, after so many hours, they knew this patient should urinate. So they got and gave him the urinal, and tried to place it for him, and everything, and tried to make, he was a Swede man. He didn't talk English very much, just a little. And these girls couldn't talk Swedish.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So they had an awful time with this poor old man. And so finally, the old man caught on.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: He said, did you want me to throw the water?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And they said yes, yes, yes. And he said, well fine, I can do that. Throw the water. Yeah, but first he told them, he said, shame on you girls playing with an old man like me.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So you know what. You get in all kinds of positions sometimes. I know, and you know, when, well, Dr. Gritman wasn't at all well in the days when I was there.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And so you remember Albert Johnson?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yeah. And I had to get, I had to catheterized him. Yeah, I catheterized him too.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But we didn't have indwelling catheters, which was, wasn't that terrible?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: I love them. We let him get so, he was paralyzed. He was only 18 and he was in a car accident and he was paralyzed. And so here we would wait till he just couldn't wait any longer, you know. And then we were irritating him with this catheter all the time. And here the poor thing and all night. And then so we, we catheterized him. And one other fellow came in one night, he was about 40. And he was so, I don't know why he couldn't avoid, but he was so uncomfortable. And so I went and asked him what to do about it. And he said, well, cath him. And so I did. And I was 23 then and he was about 40, but that man was so grateful to be relieved that it didn't mean a thing to him nor me either. It didn't, no. He was so grateful. But now the girls, they would no more think of catheterizing anybody than anything. I had only catheterized Albert once and that was all. I think I did some other old man. I wouldn't have any trouble doing it. No, but.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: There was no great shyness that you had to overcome?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, actually, because I don't know. The person didn't have any feelings. You remove yourself from all this, just as you do what you're taught. And your attitude gives them the right attitude, you see.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, sure.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And Albert, we had him there a long time. He was a nice person. Oh, Albert's lovely. He lived just this couple years ago. Yeah, he just died a couple years ago. He was always so happy to see you anytime. Oh, yes, he was so nice. And he'd been paralyzed since he was 18. As I remember him, we would always talk about it. I could just see it like it was yesterday when they brought him in. It was Labor Day and they sideswiped a car coming in from the country. They brought him in and I was walking out of his room and he had this projectile vomiting and he vomited all, they'd had a big dinner and stuff. He vomited all down my back. And just, I had carrots and everything all undigested down my back. I was like one fellow that, he was a surgical person and I was giving him bath the next morning. And I had kind of turned him over part way. I was gonna wash part of his back and then turn him the other way, you know. Turned him towards me and he got sick and he vomited everything down.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Right down your neck, huh?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Right down my neck. Yeah, I had to go and change uniforms before I could finish them. And the odors that you have to put up with, but you know, you had - And he was a service man, mind you. You know how cute they get sometimes. Yeah, they get cute. I've never had anybody really have, too cute.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: We had that old man that was the, oh, what was his name?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: He had the drug store. That was during your time at the hospital. He'd always take all of his clothes off and prance around upstairs there.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Was it Beach?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, I don't know. He had the owl, I think, for many years.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And he was just - What, was it Bulls?

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: No, no. Charlie had - It wasn't Bulls.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, Bulls was at his - Was it Hodgins?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, no. I had Mrs. Hodgins once. And she told me all about, you know, the old man that came out and did his exercises there every morning alongside the old hospital there that had him. See, we didn't tell her about the women that had the pleasure of seeing Anthony. Well, that's it, Anthony. Mrs. Hodgins told me all about Anthony. And Anthony would come out there, but he had shorts on and he would do his exercises. He had a little walkway. But he slept out there, too.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, did he?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. I don't think he did. He built, well, you would thought it looked something like - Like where Yellowstone is. Yeah, it looked like a coffin. But all the maternity, all the maternity women were on this side of the hospital. And unless, if they had their shade up, they could see this old man come out and go to bed. But of course, he had, at that time he was wearing a long knife. Now, he was in university. He had a degree. Oh, he was smart fellow. And he had a machine shop. And he could make anything for you you wanted made. I made Dr. Gritmer's thing for him. You know, his wife died. And at that time, they must not have paid much attention. I suppose maybe they embalmed her. But he kept her here for a year until he could go east to bury her. That's like they did over at Virginia City, Montana. The woman, the man died in the, this is getting off the subject of the hospital, but the man died in the winter, or he was shot in the winter. And they had these, just these little tin bathtubs. And so she put him in this bathtub and she bought enough whiskey to cover him. And she pickled him in there. And then she took him in the spring to Salt Lake City and buried him. Well, some of these women would get an awful kick out of, he would come up and he would put this lid up. You see, he left one end open so she could get the air. Well, he was a health nut. Yes, that's right. Oh, he took his exercises out there. I'd see him when I was getting the women up in the morning. And then he'd get into bed and some way or other, he must have had a string or some kind of a thing there. He would take hold and pull this lid over him and he'd be all put to bed.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Like slept in a coffin?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, kind of a coffin. Well, it was made similar. It was a bed with a lid over it, you know. But now, and he was quite an old man when he left here, he went to Montana to live. But he came back to die.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Did die here?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: He came back to Dr. Armstrong. Dr. Armstrong helped him on, you know, he never charged him anything and when he needed anything.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: He'd make it for him, huh?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yeah, he would make it for him. And before, you see, I don't know who made him. Apparently he didn't own the ground he had a shop on and he had to move it down here on Third Street someplace. Oh, that's right.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: That kind of broke his heart, didn't it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes, mm -hmm. But Mr. Anthony was a smart man.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Too late for a month and perhaps that was till 11 o 'clock.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were you allowed to date or were they watching you?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, we went out, but we had to be at that front door at 10 minutes of 10 and check in.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: For what it would happen?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, they ground you and you just didn't get any privileges. You weren't allowed after you got off duty, you know. I have been an actual nurse from the day I graduated. 52 years. See, and I've got two more years to go. And I had, as I say, about two and it was more than two. Years before that. Yeah, two years and about. 54 years you've had or plus. Four months. Well, I'd still be working today if I hadn't broken my head. Well, we've known each other for near 50 years then, Pearl. Just about, I think.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: A lot of changes in nursing, it sounds like.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, tremendous. I really don't know the changes because I haven't been in the hospital. No, she hasn't, but see, I have done, till eight years ago I worked.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What do you think was the major change, made the major difference in nursing and care?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, I think when we started our team leader thing, that was one of the, that was one of the things. I wonder whose idea that was. I wouldn't know. I think it came with, probably somebody who came in and then someplace else. I don't know about that because that was one of the major changes as far as I was concerned. Of course, now, nurses learned a lot more things. They learned how to read EKGs and to do many sophisticated things that you see. We were never taught, which it was at my age then about the time I quit, well then you see, this was a hardship really on me. It didn't hurry me to quit, but I never learned then to really, to be sure that I was, here's some of my EKG stuff yet. But I didn't, some of these things, I just went on about my way and I actually don't think they expected all these changes of me really. Well, I myself wouldn't be satisfied to have just a nurse read an EKG for me. Well, I wouldn't want it. And I don't think, I don't know how much they have pursued that, but it was really very difficult for me to take up something like this and be working on it and be worrying about if I could interpret this thing. It was too late. It was too late for me to take in it. And I don't know how much they pushed that now. If I'm gonna pay for an EKG, I'd like a doctor, a cardiologist to read it. You were supposed to be able to tell various things by this EKG of how the patient's condition was. Well, as you say, you'd want somebody that, that you knew that they knew what they were doing. And I know that most of them never, I wouldn't feel that they were qualified to really do that. But then of course we took a sample. I knew enough to take off the sample and put it on the chart and the time and the date and when the doctor could look at it when he came in to see what the changes were. And then if things looked too bad and too scrambly, well, you could always get somebody that did know how to read them, you know, to tell if you should do. But I suppose I really didn't absorb as many of the things, but I did not like teen nursing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Which was two of you would be responsible?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, just one. I would be the responsible party. And then I maybe would have four people under me that I would assign to do all the things that needed doing. And, you know, sometimes there's some things you felt that if you could just get your own hand in until you could get better results, like a return flow or something for all, you know. Yeah, oh yeah. After, Mary and I used to say, well, have Mrs. Lyon give that enema.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, there's a lot of people, a lot of nurses don't know how to give an enema properly. Now this hospital here has not been of training, did not graduate any more nurses after 1928.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why is that?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, they just quit training nurses after that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And how long had they been training before that time?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, from the time he bought the house.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh, was that right?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: In 1892. Mrs. Gritman kept a diary every day of her life. I don't know when she started, but it would have been invaluable.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But when she died, it went with her things to her relatives, didn't it, Pearl?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Yes. And it was lost. And it would have been invaluable because she kept a very detailed diary. I wrote to her nephew one time who I thought he was the last one living and I thought he would have it, but I never heard from him. He never answered. And responsible, individual.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: But she used to read to me like, well now for instance, maybe I'd only have six patients to look at during the night, don't you see?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: So there would be time.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And she was a lady that went to bed around one o 'clock, didn't she, Pearl?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yes. Always up. And maybe she'd been out playing bridge or something. But when she came in oftentimes she'd read some of her diary. She told me lots of things about the early times here, as she would go and go back and look in her diary. And so I knew that she had this wonderful diary.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So it wasn't just about things going on in the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No, no. It was general things. And I thought, well, it was just too bad that it was lost because it would have just told so many. Well, it - It was detailed. She would lots of times jot down who was operated that morning. Oh yes, yes. And, but she didn't ever put down what they were operated for, but they had surgery that morning. They had surgery.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Would she assist, would she nurse at times?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: No. No, she didn't actually take, I mean, give a patient a bath or anything like that. But she was always around to greeting them every morning. And maybe lots of times in the evenings. And she, as I said, she had charged, she planned all the meals. And she had charge of ordering the food.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Did we tell how much canning and stuff they did during the summer? Well, I don't - At the hospital?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Oh yes. Did you - When I came there, fruit, lovely, lovely fruit and stuff that they canned down there in the kitchen. Oh yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And so they would put up enough food on the premises to feed the hospital patients?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Well, perhaps food. Everybody. Everybody that ate there. Because there would be the janitor and they always had a university boy when I was there.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: And he was somebody who was interested in being a doctor usually, wasn't it?

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Ryerson, since Spokane was the last one I remember, Pete Ryerson. Pete Ryerson. Hope he would tell us the awfulest stories you ever - He just deviled us to death. He was a rascal.

speaker: Pauline (Pearl) Maclied or Florence Melder Lange
words: Now, who did he, is he a pediatrician?
